items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Urgency of Acute Intestinal Obstruction
items.content: The incidence of acute intestinal obstruction has increased, but morbidity and mortality are decreasing. The extent of mechanical obstruction is critical for determining urgency: partial, high grade, or complete. Strangulated obstructions indicate vascular insufficiency and require immediate attention.
keyPoints: Acute intestinal obstruction accounts for ~1–3% of all hospitalizations.
Approximately 80% involve the small bowel; one-third show significant ischemia.
Mortality rate is ~8% if operated within 24-30 hours, tripling thereafter.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.title: Causes and Management of Mechanical Bowel Obstruction
items.content: Mechanical bowel obstruction can be caused by extrinsic processes like adhesions or hernias, intrinsic abnormalities such as neoplasms or congenital issues, and intraluminal factors like bezoars. Postoperative adhesions are the most common cause in developed countries.
keyPoints: Adhesions account for most early postoperative obstructions.
20% of conservatively treated patients may need readmission within 10 years.
Laparoscopic procedures reduce but do not eliminate adhesion risk.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.title: Identifying and Addressing Volvulus in Acute Intestinal Obstruction
items.content: Volvulus, a twisting of the bowel on its mesenteric axis, can lead to partial or complete obstruction with vascular insufficiency. It is more common in the sigmoid colon but can also affect the cecum and terminal ileum.
keyPoints: Sigmoid volvulus accounts for ~4% of large-bowel obstructions.
Risk factors include institutionalization, neuropsychiatric conditions, chronic constipation, and aging.
More common in Eastern Europe, Russia, and Africa than the U.S.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.title: Mechanism of Bowel Wall Hypoxemia and Necrosis in Intestinal Obstruction
items.content: In acute intestinal obstruction, increased intraluminal pressure can exceed venous pressure, impeding venous and lymphatic drainage. This leads to edema and hypoxemia of the bowel wall proximal to the blockage, potentially resulting in epithelial necrosis within 12 hours.
keyPoints: Increased intestinal contractility occurs both proximally and distally to the obstruction.
Intraluminal pressure exceeding venous pressure impedes drainage, causing edema and hypoxemia.
Epithelial necrosis can occur within 12 hours of obstruction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Risks and Consequences of Closed-Loop Obstruction
items.content: Closed-loop obstruction, where both proximal and distal openings of a bowel segment are occluded, significantly increases the risk of vascular insufficiency, systemic inflammation, hemodynamic compromise, and irreversible ischemia. Emergent intervention is crucial as pathologic changes can progress rapidly.
keyPoints: Closed-loop obstructions are common precursors to strangulation.
Risk of severe complications like vascular insufficiency and systemic inflammation is high.
Cecal diameter exceeding 10–12 cm in high-grade distal colonic obstruction with competent ileocecal valves can lead to ischemic necrosis and perforation.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Early Signs and Symptoms of Acute Intestinal Obstruction
items.content: A thorough history and physical examination can accurately diagnose acute intestinal obstruction, even before imaging. Recognizing early signs such as colicky abdominal pain, distention, emesis, and obstipation is crucial for prompt treatment to reduce morbidity and mortality.
keyPoints: Cardinal signs include colicky abdominal pain, abdominal distention, emesis, and obstipation.
Distal obstruction leads to greater distention and delayed feculent emesis due to bacterial overgrowth.
Proximal obstruction presents with less distention but more pronounced vomiting.
History should consider prior surgery, cancer, or inflammatory bowel disease.
Patients may appear critically ill, oliguric, hypotensive, and tachycardic from volume depletion.
Fever suggests strangulation or systemic inflammation.
Early small-bowel obstruction often presents with high-pitched, musical bowel sounds and borborygmi.
Later stages may show absent or hypoactive bowel sounds.
Partial blockage allows flatus and stool passage; complete blockage may evacuate contents downstream.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL FINDINGS

items.title: Differentiating Between Mechanical Obstruction and Ileus/Pseudo-obstruction
items.content: Distinguishing between mechanical bowel obstruction and ileus or pseudo-obstruction is essential. Key differences include the presence of colicky pain, emesis, and ongoing discharge of stool or flatus in mechanical obstructions, whereas ileus typically lacks these symptoms.
keyPoints: Mechanical obstruction presents with colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and obstipation.
Ileus or pseudo-obstruction may mimic bowel obstruction but usually lacks colicky pain and emesis.
Ongoing discharge of stool or flatus can help differentiate ileus from complete mechanical obstruction.
Laparoscopic procedures have a lower risk of ileus compared to other surgical approaches.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL FINDINGS

items.title: Laboratory Indicators of Bowel Obstruction Severity and Malignancy
items.content: Laboratory tests for acute intestinal obstruction include complete blood count, serum electrolytes, and creatinine. Mild hemoconcentration and slight leukocytosis are common in simple bowel obstructions. Severe volume depletion or ischemic necrosis may present with high white cell counts, immature forms, and metabolic acidosis. Guaiac-positive stools and iron-deficiency anemia suggest malignancy.
keyPoints: Mild hemoconcentration and slight leukocytosis in simple bowel obstruction
Hypokalemia, hypochloremia, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratios, metabolic alkalosis due to emesis and dehydration
Hyponatremia from rehydration with hypotonic fluids
Guaiac-positive stools and iron-deficiency anemia suggest malignancy
High WBC count with immature forms and metabolic acidosis indicate severe volume depletion or ischemic necrosis
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.title: Imaging Modalities for Diagnosing Bowel Obstruction
items.content: Abdominal radiography is quick and can suggest the need for emergency surgery. A 'staircasing' pattern in small-bowel obstruction shows dilated loops >2.5 cm with little or no air in the colon. CT imaging has high sensitivity (~95%) and specificity (96%) for detecting bowel obstruction, but lower accuracy for closed-loop obstructions (60%). CT can identify ischemia through altered bowel wall enhancement.
keyPoints: Abdominal radiography includes upright/cross-table lateral views
'Staircasing' pattern with loops >2.5 cm in diameter indicates small-bowel obstruction
CT sensitivity for high-grade obstruction ~95%, specificity 96%
CT accuracy lower for closed-loop obstruction (60%)
Altered bowel wall enhancement on CT suggests ischemia
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.title: Use of Contrast Studies in Bowel Obstruction Diagnosis
items.content: Contrast studies can distinguish between ileus and obstruction. A 'bird's beak' or 'whorl' deformity on CT indicates colonic volvulus. Barium studies are contraindicated in complete or high-grade obstructions due to risk of barium concretions and interference with further imaging.
keyPoints: Contrast studies differentiate ileus from obstruction
'Bird's beak' or 'whorl' deformity on CT suggests colonic volvulus
Barium studies contraindicated in complete/high-grade obstructions
Risk of barium concretions and interference with further imaging
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.subtopic: Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)
items.title: Management of Acute Bowel Obstruction
items.content: Stabilization is crucial in acute bowel obstruction, involving fluid resuscitation, electrolyte repletion, and nasogastric decompression to minimize distention and reduce aspiration risk. Antibiotics may be used prophylactically if surgery is anticipated.
keyPoints: Fluid resuscitation and electrolyte repletion are essential for stabilization.
Nasogastric tube suction decompresses the stomach, improving comfort and reducing aspiration risk.
Antibiotic use is controversial but may be warranted prophylactically if surgery is anticipated.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)

items.subtopic: Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)
items.title: Treatment of Colonic Pseudo-Obstruction with Neostigmine
items.content: Neostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, is used to treat colonic pseudo-obstruction by increasing cholinergic activity and stimulating colonic motility. It requires cardiac monitoring and atropine availability.
keyPoints: Neostigmine increases cholinergic activity, stimulating colonic motility.
Cardiac monitoring is required during neostigmine administration.
Atropine should be immediately available to manage potential bradycardia.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)

items.subtopic: VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS
items.title: Management of Sigmoid and Cecal Volvulus
items.content: Sigmoid volvulus can often be managed with non-surgical decompression using a flexible tube through a rigid proctoscope or flexible sigmoidoscope, allowing for elective definitive correction. In contrast, cecal volvulus typically requires surgical intervention via laparotomy or laparoscopic correction.
keyPoints: Sigmoid volvulus can often be managed non-surgically with decompression using a flexible tube through a proctoscope or sigmoidoscope.
Cecal volvulus most often necessitates surgical correction (laparotomy or laparoscopy).
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS

items.subtopic: VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS
items.title: Approach to Adult Intussusception and Gallstone Ileus
items.content: In adult intussusception, primary resection is recommended. Manual reduction of involved bowel can minimize the extent of resection needed, with a proximal ostomy required if unprepped colon is involved. For gallstone ileus, which most commonly obstructs the ileum in 60% of cases, operative enterolithotomy is used to remove the stone via a cholecystoduodenal fistula.
keyPoints: Primary resection is recommended for adult intussusception; manual reduction may limit necessary bowel removal.
A proximal ostomy might be needed if unprepped colon is involved in intussusception.
Gallstone ileus most commonly obstructs the ileum (60% of cases).
Operative enterolithotomy is used to remove gallstones that have entered via a cholecystoduodenal fistula.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS

items.title: Identifying True Postoperative Small-Bowel Obstruction
items.content: In patients who initially experience postoperative ileus and then develop obstructive symptoms after a return to normal bowel function, there is a higher likelihood of true small-bowel obstruction.
keyPoints: Obstruction occurring within the first 6 weeks post-operation is considered early postoperative mechanical bowel obstruction.
Patients with initial ileus followed by obstructive symptoms are more likely to have true postoperative small-bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · POSTOPERATIVE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION

items.title: Timing and Surgical Intervention in Postoperative Bowel Obstruction
items.content: The duration of obstructive symptoms after hospitalization is a critical factor; prolonged symptoms increase the likelihood that surgical intervention will be necessary.
keyPoints: Obstructive symptoms persisting longer post-hospitalization indicate a higher need for surgical intervention.
Early postoperative mechanical bowel obstruction occurs within 6 weeks and may resolve spontaneously, but persistent symptoms suggest surgery.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · POSTOPERATIVE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: What is the most common cause of mechanical obstruction in the small intestine in the United States and Europe?
items.explanation: Postoperative adhesions are responsible for most cases of mechanical obstruction in the small intestine in these regions.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.question: Which type of volvulus is most common and what percentage of large-bowel obstructions does it account for?
items.explanation: Volvulus affecting the sigmoid colon is most common and accounts for approximately two-thirds of all cases of large-bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.question: What is the mortality rate for patients with strangulated intestinal obstruction operated on within 24–30 hours?
items.explanation: The mortality rate is approximately 8% if the patient is operated on within 24-30 hours.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.question: What percentage of patients with small bowel obstruction show evidence of significant ischemia?
items.explanation: About one-third, or approximately 30%, of small bowel obstruction cases show evidence of significant ischemia.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction

items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for intestinal distension during acute intestinal obstruction?
options: Increased peristalsis proximal to the obstruction
items.explanation: Swallowed air is a primary source of intestinal distension, along with accumulation from fermentation.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What occurs when luminal pressure exceeds venous pressure during acute intestinal obstruction?
items.explanation: When luminal pressure exceeds venous pressure, venous and lymphatic drainage is impeded.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: What can result from compromised arterial blood supply in acute intestinal obstruction?
items.explanation: Compromised arterial blood supply may lead to full-thickness ischemia, necrosis, and perforation.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.question: Which symptom is more pronounced in patients with proximal small-bowel obstruction?
items.explanation: Patients with more proximal obstruction commonly present with less abdominal distention but more pronounced vomiting.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL FINDINGS

items.question: What is a classic finding in early small-bowel obstruction?
options: Musical tinkling bowel sounds and borborygmi
Severe abdominal pain with peritoneal irritation
Ongoing discharge of stool or flatus
items.explanation: Many patients with early small-bowel obstruction will have high-pitched, 'musical' tinkling bowel sounds and peristaltic 'rushes' known as borborygmi.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL FINDINGS

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature between ileus or pseudo-obstruction and complete mechanical bowel obstruction?
options: Ongoing discharge of stool or flatus
items.explanation: Patients with ileus may not have nausea or emesis, and ongoing discharge of stool or flatus can help distinguish them from those with complete mechanical bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · HISTORY AND PHYSICAL FINDINGS

items.question: Which laboratory finding is strongly suggestive of malignancy in patients with intestinal obstruction?
items.explanation: The presence of guaiac-positive stools and iron-deficiency anemia are strongly suggestive of malignancy.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.question: What is a classical finding on abdominal radiography in patients with small-bowel obstruction?
options: Staircasing pattern of dilated air and fluid-filled small-bowel loops >2.5 cm
Little bowel gas in the colon
items.explanation: A 'staircasing' pattern of dilated air and fluid-filled small-bowel loops >2.5 cm is a classical finding in patients with small-bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.question: Which imaging modality has the highest sensitivity for detecting bowel obstruction?
items.explanation: Computed tomography (CT) is the most frequently used imaging modality with a sensitivity of ~95% for detecting bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.question: Why are barium studies generally contraindicated in patients with complete or high-grade bowel obstruction?
options: Barium may lead to the development of concretions causing additional blockage
Barium reduces sensitivity for detecting ischemia
Barium is less effective than CT imaging
items.explanation: Patients with significant obstruction may develop barium concretions as an additional source of blockage.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.subtopic: Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)
items.question: What is the primary purpose of nasogastric tube suction in patients with acute intestinal obstruction?
options: To decompress the stomach and reduce the risk of aspiration
To administer antibiotics directly to the stomach
items.explanation: Nasogastric tube suction decompresses the stomach, minimizes further distention from swallowed air, improves patient comfort, and reduces the risk of aspiration.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)

items.subtopic: Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)
items.question: Which pharmacologic treatment is commonly used for ileus to stimulate gastrointestinal recovery?
items.explanation: Treatment with peripherally active $ μ $-opioid receptor antagonists (e.g., alvimopan and methylnaltrexone) or 5-HT4 agonists that stimulate the release of acetylcholine from enteric neurons may accelerate gastrointestinal recovery in some patients who have undergone abdominal surgery.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)

items.subtopic: Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)
items.question: What is a necessary precaution when administering neostigmine for colonic pseudo-obstruction?
options: Ensure there is no mechanical obstruction
items.explanation: Neostigmine can be used once it is certain that there is no mechanical obstruction. Cardiac monitoring is required, and atropine should be immediately available.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · Acute Intestinal Obstruction | ILEUS | COLONIC PSEUDO-OBSTRUCTION (OGILVIE'S DISEASE)

items.subtopic: VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS
items.question: Which procedure is most often required for cecal volvulus?
items.explanation: Cecal volvulus most often requires surgical intervention, either by laparotomy or laparoscopic correction.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS

items.subtopic: VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS
items.question: What is the defining feature of bowel obstruction observed at operation?
options: Dilation proximal to the site of blockage with distal collapse
items.explanation: The defining feature is dilation proximal to the site of blockage with distal collapse.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS

items.subtopic: VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS
items.question: What is the most common site of intestinal obstruction in patients with gallstone ileus?
items.explanation: The most common site of intestinal obstruction in patients with gallstone ileus is the ileum.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · VOLVULUS | INTRAOPERATIVE STRATEGIES | ADULT INTUSSUSCEPTION AND GALLSTONE ILEUS

items.question: What time frame defines early postoperative mechanical bowel obstruction?
options: Within the first 6 weeks of operation
Within the first month after surgery
After 6 weeks but within 3 months of operation
items.explanation: Early postoperative mechanical bowel obstruction is defined as occurring within the first 6 weeks of operation.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · POSTOPERATIVE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: Which patients should have a higher index of suspicion for a definitive site of obstruction?
options: Patients with classic mechanical bowel obstruction
Patients who undergo laparoscopic surgical procedures
Patients with no prior history of bowel issues
items.explanation: A higher index of suspicion for a definitive site of obstruction is warranted for patients who undergo laparoscopic surgical procedures.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · POSTOPERATIVE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION

items.question: What increases the likelihood that a patient will require surgical intervention?
options: Obstructive symptoms resolving quickly after hospitalization
A history of postoperative ileus without obstruction
The longer it takes for obstructive symptoms to resolve after hospitalization
Symptoms occurring within the first week post-surgery
items.explanation: The longer it takes for a patient's obstructive symptoms to resolve after hospitalization, the more likely they are to require surgical intervention.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · POSTOPERATIVE BOWEL OBSTRUCTION

items.statement: Adhesions are the most common cause of early postoperative bowel obstruction.
items.explanation: Adhesions account for the majority of cases requiring intervention after early postoperative periods.

items.statement: Primary small-bowel cancer is a common cause of intestinal obstruction.
items.explanation: Primary small-bowel cancer is rare as a cause of intestinal obstruction.

items.statement: Inflammatory edema decreases the production of reactive oxygen species.
items.explanation: Inflammatory edema increases the production of reactive oxygen species.

items.statement: Closed-loop obstruction is a common precursor for strangulation due to occlusion at both ends of a bowel segment.
items.explanation: Closed-loop obstruction, where both ends are occluded, is the most common precursor for strangulation.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.statement: Bowel sounds are typically absent from the beginning in patients with small-bowel obstruction.

items.assertion: Postoperative ileus is a common reason for delayed hospital discharge.
items.reason: Ileus often occurs after intraabdominal surgery and can be transient, delaying patient recovery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why postoperative ileus delays discharge.

items.assertion: Volvulus is most commonly caused by adhesions or hernias.
items.reason: Adhesions or hernias are rare causes of colonic volvulus.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because volvulus is not primarily caused by adhesions or hernias; the reason is true.

items.assertion: Patients with partial blockage may continue to pass flatus and stool.
items.reason: This is because the obstruction does not completely prevent passage of gas or stool downstream.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Increased serum D-lactate levels may suggest strangulated bowel obstruction.
items.reason: No laboratory tests are especially useful for identifying the presence of simple or strangulated obstruction, but increases in serum D-lactate levels may be suggestive of the latter.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Acute Intestinal Obstruction · LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES

items.assertion: Abdominal radiography can accurately distinguish obstruction from other causes of colonic dysmotility.
items.reason: Abdominal radiography may indicate the need for emergency surgical intervention in patients with high-grade or complete obstruction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because abdominal radiography may not accurately distinguish obstruction from other causes of colonic dysmotility.
